T-Soft.history: T-Soft is a Turkish SaaS ecommerce platform founded around 2002 in Turkey, providing B2B and B2C online store capabilities primarily to Turkish market customers.
T-Soft.summary_abuse: T-Soft has no notable abuse signal. It is a regional ecommerce platform serving Turkish merchants with no documented pattern of exploitation for fraud.

T1 Comercios.history: T1 Comercios is a marketplace integration platform for Mexican ecommerce, allowing merchants to sync with Claroshop, Sears Mexico, and Sanborns. It appears to be part of the Claro/Telmex digital commerce ecosystem in Mexico.
T1 Comercios.summary_abuse: T1 Comercios has no notable abuse signal. It is a regional marketplace integration tool for Mexican retailers.

T1 Envios.history: T1 Envios is the shipping/delivery component of the T1 ecommerce ecosystem launched by Arturo Elias Slim in Mexico, built as an integrated platform for SMEs. The T1 suite (Paginas, Pagos, Envios) is associated with Telmex and the Slim family's venture to democratize Mexican ecommerce.
T1 Envios.summary_abuse: T1 Envios is a Mexican shipping aggregation service for ecommerce. It has no documented abuse signal.

T1 Paginas.history: T1 Paginas is the ecommerce storefront component of the T1 platform, a Mexico-based all-in-one ecommerce ecosystem launched by Arturo Elias Slim targeting SMEs. Used by over 8,000 companies in Mexico and associated with Telmex.
T1 Paginas.summary_abuse: T1 Paginas is a Mexican ecommerce store builder for SMEs with no documented abuse signal.

T1 Pagos.history: T1 Pagos is the payment gateway component of the T1 ecommerce ecosystem, part of a Mexico-based SME platform associated with Telmex and Arturo Elias Slim. It processes credit cards, SPEI, and PayPal with an 86% approval rate.
T1 Pagos.summary_abuse: T1 Pagos is a Mexican payment processor for ecommerce. Payment processors can be misused to process fraudulent transactions, but T1 Pagos reports a low chargeback rate and is affiliated with Telmex, which reduces the risk profile.

Tabarnapp.history: Tabarnapp is a small Canadian company providing Shopify apps and themes. No notable public history or ownership events documented.
Tabarnapp.summary_abuse: Tabarnapp has no notable abuse signal. It is a Shopify app development company with no consumer-facing hosted surface exploitable for fraud.

Tabby.history: Tabby was founded in 2019 in the UAE and is a leading buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) fintech in the Middle East, operating in Saudi Arabia and UAE. It has raised significant VC funding.
Tabby.summary_abuse: Tabby is a BNPL payment service in the Middle East. Like all BNPL services, it carries some exposure to account fraud and synthetic identity abuse, but Tabby operates within regulated financial infrastructure. No specific major documented abuse incidents are known.

Tableau.history: Tableau was founded in 2003 by Chris Stolte, Pat Hanrahan, and Christian Chabot as a Stanford spin-out to commercialize research on visual analytics. It went public in 2013 and became the market-leading BI visualization tool. Salesforce acquired Tableau in 2019 for approximately $15.7 billion.
Tableau.summary_abuse: Tableau has no notable abuse signal. It is a BI and data visualization tool used within enterprise environments; there is no consumer-facing surface that scammers would exploit.

TableBooker.history: Tablebooker is a Belgian online restaurant reservation platform. Minimal public company history is available beyond its Belgian market focus.
TableBooker.summary_abuse: Tablebooker is a restaurant reservation platform with no documented abuse signal.

TableCheck.history: TableCheck was founded in Japan in 2011, launching its TableSolution table management system in 2013. The company grew to become the leading restaurant reservation platform in Japan with over 8,000 restaurant clients including 200+ Michelin-starred restaurants, and expanded to 35 countries.
TableCheck.summary_abuse: TableCheck is a restaurant reservation and table management platform with no documented abuse signal.

TableFever.history: TableFever is a restaurant discovery and reservation SaaS platform; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.
TableFever.summary_abuse: TableFever is a restaurant reservation platform with no notable abuse signal; its hospitality use case does not attract significant phishing or fraud activity.

Tableo.history: Tableo is a restaurant reservation management SaaS that optimizes table allocation and booking workflows; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.
Tableo.summary_abuse: Tableo is a restaurant reservation management system with no notable abuse signal; its hospitality vertical does not attract phishing or fraud activity.

TablePress.history: TablePress is a free WordPress plugin created by Tobias Bäthge in 2012 that allows creating and managing tables. It has been one of the most popular WordPress table plugins for over a decade with no ownership changes.
TablePress.summary_abuse: TablePress has no notable abuse signal. It is a WordPress plugin for table display with no hosted service surface.

Tablesorter.history: Tablesorter is a jQuery plugin for client-side table sorting created around 2007. It has been widely used in web applications for simple table interactions. No major ownership changes.
Tablesorter.summary_abuse: Tablesorter has no notable abuse signal. It is a client-side JavaScript table sorting library with no hosted service surface.

Taboola.history: Taboola was founded in 2007 by Adam Singolda in Israel and later headquartered in New York. It became one of the two dominant content discovery and native advertising networks (alongside Outbrain). Taboola went public via SPAC merger in 2021. A planned merger with Outbrain in 2019-2020 was abandoned.
Taboola.summary_abuse: Taboola is a large-scale native advertising and content recommendation platform embedded on major news sites. It has been widely criticized by security researchers for distributing malvertising and scam ads, including fake health supplements, phishing lures, and misleading clickbait. The heavy ad-tech tracker footprint and limited ad quality controls have historically made Taboola a common vector for scam content delivery, though the company has anti-fraud policies. The abuse risk is moderate as the platform enables advertisers to reach users on reputable publisher sites under the publisher's implicit endorsement.

Tabulator.history: Tabulator is an open-source JavaScript data grid library created by Oliver Folkerd around 2015. It provides interactive, feature-rich tables for web applications and has grown a substantial user base. Licensed under MIT with no commercial backing.
Tabulator.summary_abuse: Tabulator has no notable abuse signal. It is a client-side JavaScript data grid library with no hosted service surface.

Tachyons.history: Tachyons is a functional CSS framework created by Adam Morse around 2014. It popularized atomic/functional CSS patterns before Tailwind CSS became dominant. It has seen limited updates in recent years as Tailwind has captured most of the market.
Tachyons.summary_abuse: Tachyons has no notable abuse signal. It is a CSS utility framework with no hosted service surface.

Tactful.history: Tactful is an AI-driven omnichannel customer service platform for enterprises; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.
Tactful.summary_abuse: Tactful is an AI customer service platform with no notable abuse signal; it processes customer service interactions and has no consumer-facing surface used for phishing or fraud.

Tada.history: Tada is a Shopify app offering interactive website popups for lead capture, email collection, and spin-the-wheel promotions; no founding date or notable ownership history is documented.
Tada.summary_abuse: Tada is a Shopify popup and lead collection app with no documented abuse signal; popup tools can collect email addresses which may be abused by merchants, but the app itself is not a known fraud tool.

Tagalys.history: Tagalys was founded around 2015 by Antony Kattukaran and Palaniappan Chellapan, initially in Chennai, India, as a data-driven product discovery and visual merchandising platform for fashion ecommerce.
Tagalys.summary_abuse: Tagalys is a B2B ecommerce personalization platform with no documented abuse signal.

Tagboard.history: Tagboard was founded around 2011-2012 in Redmond, WA by Josh Decker, Tim Shimotakahara, and Sean Sperte as a hashtag aggregation and social media display platform. It serves brands, agencies, media companies, and sports organizations for live event and broadcast social media displays. In 2016, it acquired social media publisher tool Capture.
Tagboard.summary_abuse: Tagboard is a social media content aggregation platform for brands and events. It aggregates public social content, raising standard data scraping considerations, but has no documented fraud or malware abuse incidents.

Tagembed.history: Tagembed is an India-based social media aggregator platform launched around 2018, allowing websites to embed UGC feeds from Instagram, Twitter, Facebook, and other networks. No notable ownership changes are on record.
Tagembed.summary_abuse: Tagembed aggregates public social media content for display on websites. Social media embedding tools can be used to amplify misleading content if unvetted, but no specific documented abuse incidents exist for this vendor.

Taggbox.history: Taggbox is an India-based UGC platform launched around 2018, enabling brands to collect, curate, manage rights, and display user-generated content from social media across websites, events, and digital signage. No notable ownership changes on record.
Taggbox.summary_abuse: Taggbox is a B2B UGC platform that aggregates social media content for brands. No specific documented abuse incidents exist, though UGC tools require proper rights management to avoid displaying infringing content.

Tagnology.history: Tagnology is a UGC (user-generated content) and social interaction platform for brands; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
Tagnology.summary_abuse: Tagnology is a social content platform with no notable abuse signal; it is used by brands to collect and display UGC without documented involvement in phishing or fraud.

TagPro.history: TagPro is a tag management and website tag updating tool from AdProMedia; no founding date or notable ownership changes are publicly documented.
TagPro.summary_abuse: TagPro is a tag management tool with no notable abuse signal; tag managers are occasionally abused via supply-chain injection, but no specific incidents are documented for this vendor.

Tagshop.history: Tagshop is an AI-powered shoppable content SaaS that integrates social content and UGC into ecommerce storefronts; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
Tagshop.summary_abuse: Tagshop is a shoppable social content platform with no notable abuse signal; it is used by ecommerce brands to display UGC product content without documented fraud involvement.

Tagtoo.history: Tagtoo is a Taiwanese digital advertising platform providing retargeting and programmatic ad solutions. Minimal public company founding history is available beyond its Asia-Pacific focus.
Tagtoo.summary_abuse: Tagtoo is a digital advertising and retargeting platform. Ad-tech platforms collect behavioral data at scale for targeting purposes. No specific documented fraud or malware abuse incidents are on record.

Taiga.history: Taiga was created by Kaleidos, a Spanish software company, and launched in 2014 as an open-source agile project management tool competing with Jira and Trello. In 2021, development was transitioned to a community-led model. The cloud version continues to operate alongside the self-hosted open-source edition.
Taiga.summary_abuse: Taiga has no notable abuse signal. It is an open-source project management tool with no consumer-facing surface exploitable for fraud.

Tail.history: Tail is a customer data management platform (tail.digital) focused on data unification for retail and CPG; it appears to be a Brazilian company; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
Tail.summary_abuse: Tail is a customer data platform with no notable abuse signal; it processes first-party customer data for brands without documented involvement in fraud.

Tailwind CSS.history: Tailwind CSS was created by Adam Wathan and released in 2017. It pioneered the utility-first CSS approach and rapidly became the dominant CSS framework for modern web development. Adam Wathan founded Tailwind Labs with Steve Schoger to commercialize components and tooling around the open-source framework.
Tailwind CSS.summary_abuse: Tailwind CSS has no notable abuse signal. It is a CSS utility framework with no hosted service surface.

TakeDrop.history: TakeDrop is a Polish ecommerce dropshipping platform founded around 2017 in Wroclaw. It secured a seed round of €1.1 million in September 2023, backed by IAI.pl co-founder Pawel Fornalski. No notable ownership changes since founding.
TakeDrop.summary_abuse: TakeDrop is a small Polish dropshipping and ecommerce platform. Like any ecommerce builder, it could in principle be used to create scam storefronts, but there is no documented pattern of widespread abuse specific to TakeDrop given its small market footprint.

TakeTheme.history: TakeTheme is an Egyptian e-commerce SaaS platform targeting Egyptian and Gulf region merchants, offering zero-commission online store creation with integrated payment gateways and shipping.
TakeTheme.summary_abuse: TakeTheme is a regional e-commerce platform primarily serving Egyptian and Gulf markets. It enables merchants to create online storefronts, which carries a low but nonzero risk of fake storefront creation. No documented abuse incidents are publicly known; its limited geographic scope reduces overall exposure.

Talent Clue.history: Talent Clue is a Spanish inbound recruitment software platform, helping organizations attract candidates through employer branding. It has been active since approximately 2012.
Talent Clue.summary_abuse: Talent Clue is a recruitment software platform with no documented abuse signal.

TalentBrew.history: TalentBrew is a UK-based recruitment SaaS platform for streamlining hiring processes; no founding date or notable ownership changes are publicly documented.
TalentBrew.summary_abuse: TalentBrew is a UK recruitment platform with no notable abuse signal; it serves HR teams for legitimate hiring workflows without documented fraud involvement.

Talentegy.history: Talentegy was founded in 2017 by Stephanie Ralston, Dwaine Maltais, and Shawna Berthold as an AI-enhanced talent analytics platform for optimizing candidate and employee experience. In September 2020, Talentegy was acquired by Jobvite, a recruitment marketing platform company, as part of Jobvite's strategy to enhance its talent acquisition analytics capabilities.
Talentegy.summary_abuse: Talentegy is a B2B talent analytics and recruiting experience platform used by HR professionals, with no consumer-facing sign-up or content hosting surface. Its product processes HR data for corporate recruiting workflows. No abuse, phishing, or fraud incidents involving Talentegy have been documented.

Talention.history: Talention was founded in 2011 in Wuppertal, Germany, by Steffen Braun and Christian Ternai, whose collaboration began as research in 2010 at the University of Wuppertal's Faculty for Business Development and Innovation, supported by Germany's Federal Ministry of Economics and Technology. The platform specializes in data-driven recruitment marketing tools for corporate hiring teams.
Talention.summary_abuse: Talention is a B2B recruitment marketing SaaS used by HR and talent acquisition teams. Its user base is corporate clients, and the platform processes job posting campaigns and candidate tracking data. No consumer-facing content hosting and no documented abuse incidents make it a low-risk platform.

Talex.history: Talex AB is a Swedish ecommerce and e-learning platform provider founded in 2002 (with roots as a private business from 1997). The company is based in Sweden and serves over 1,400 customers in the Scandinavian market.
Talex.summary_abuse: Talex is a niche Swedish ecommerce platform with no documented abuse signal. Its limited geographic reach and subscription-based model with no anonymous free tier do not present elevated abuse risk.

Talk-Me.history: Talk-Me is a Russian-developed live chat and customer communication platform operating at talk-me.ru. It targets small and medium businesses looking to add chat support to their websites, offering a freemium model.
Talk-Me.summary_abuse: Talk-Me is a small Russian live chat platform primarily serving Russian-language websites. The tool embeds a chat widget on customer websites and collects visitor data server-side in Russia, presenting data residency and privacy concerns for non-Russian users. No specific documented abuse incidents are publicly known, but its Russian hosting jurisdiction limits oversight.

Talkable.history: Talkable was founded in 2010 (originally as Curebit) and is based in the United States. The company pivoted to become a referral marketing platform for mid-size to large ecommerce businesses, backed by Y Combinator.
Talkable.summary_abuse: Talkable is a B2B referral marketing platform used by ecommerce businesses. It injects referral widgets and tracking scripts on merchant sites and collects customer data for referral attribution. The platform is used legitimately; no notable fraud or phishing abuse is documented, though its referral tracking involves PII collection from shoppers.

Talkdesk.history: Talkdesk was founded in 2011 in Portugal and later headquartered in San Francisco. It grew to become a major cloud contact center platform, achieving unicorn status with over $2.7 billion valuation after significant VC funding rounds.
Talkdesk.summary_abuse: Talkdesk has no notable abuse signal. It is an enterprise cloud contact center platform used by businesses for customer service operations.

TalkJS.history: Founded in 2016 and based in Eindhoven, Netherlands, TalkJS provides a chat API and SDK enabling developers to add user-to-user messaging features to marketplaces and platforms; no notable acquisitions or ownership changes are documented.
TalkJS.summary_abuse: TalkJS is a developer API for embedding messaging in third-party platforms, with no notable abuse signal. Its usage is B2B and API-driven, meaning abusive use would require a developer integration; TalkJS itself is not a consumer-facing platform.

Tallentor.history: Tallentor is a subscription-based website analytics and heatmap tool; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.
Tallentor.summary_abuse: Tallentor is a website analytics tool with no notable abuse signal; it is a passive measurement service with no consumer-facing surface attracting fraud.

Tallentor Widget.history: Tallentor Widget is the embeddable widget component of the Tallentor website analytics and heatmap platform; both are offered by the same vendor at tallentor.com.
Tallentor Widget.summary_abuse: Tallentor Widget is an embeddable analytics widget with no notable abuse signal; it is a passive data collection component with no features attractive to phishing or fraud actors.

Tally.history: Tally was founded in 2020 in Belgium by Filip Minev and Marie Martens as a simple, free form builder. It grew quickly as an alternative to Typeform and Google Forms. No major ownership changes.
Tally.summary_abuse: Tally is a form builder with a free tier that does not require custom domains. Like all form builders, it could theoretically be used to create phishing forms, but the free tier is relatively constrained and no documented large-scale abuse pattern is known.

Tamago.history: Tamago (previously known as Tamago Cart) is a subscription and repeat-purchase ecommerce platform developed by Temonalab, a company based in Japan. The platform specializes in recurring billing and subscription-box management for Japanese ecommerce merchants. Limited English-language historical information is publicly available about the company's founding year or corporate milestones.
Tamago.summary_abuse: Tamago is a Japanese subscription billing ecommerce platform serving Japanese merchants. Its use case is managing legitimate recurring purchase businesses. No documented fraud or phishing incidents involving Tamago have been publicly reported in English-language sources.

Tamara.history: Tamara was founded in 2020 in Saudi Arabia as a BNPL platform focused on the Middle East. It has grown to be one of the leading BNPL providers in Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Kuwait with significant VC backing.
Tamara.summary_abuse: Tamara is a BNPL fintech operating within regulated financial infrastructure in the Middle East. Like all BNPL services, it carries exposure to account fraud and synthetic identity abuse, but no specific documented large-scale incidents are known.

Tangiblee.history: Founded in 2014 and headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, Tangiblee provides an enterprise augmented-reality and immersive shopping platform for ecommerce brands; the company is bootstrapped with $9.4M in revenue and 71 employees as of the mid-2020s with no notable acquisitions.
Tangiblee.summary_abuse: Tangiblee is an enterprise AR ecommerce platform sold directly to retailers with no notable abuse signal; it has no consumer-facing surface that would attract fraudsters.

Tangled Network.history: Tangled Network is a managed services provider for website development and data hosting; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.
Tangled Network.summary_abuse: Tangled Network is a managed web and data services provider with no notable abuse signal; it operates as a B2B service without documented involvement in phishing or fraud.

TanStack.history: TanStack was created by Tanner Linsley, starting with TanStack Query (React Query) around 2019-2020, which became one of the most popular React data-fetching libraries. The project expanded into TanStack Table, Router, Form, and other headless utilities, all MIT-licensed.
TanStack.summary_abuse: TanStack has no notable abuse signal. It is a collection of open-source headless JavaScript libraries for UI development.

Tanx.history: Tanx (Taobao Ad Network and Exchange) was developed by Alibaba Group in China as part of its advertising ecosystem, merging Alimama with the Taobao Affiliate network. It operates as one of the earliest RTB platforms in the Chinese programmatic advertising market, offering ad exchange, DSP, SSP and DMP capabilities.
Tanx.summary_abuse: Tanx is an ad exchange platform operated by Alibaba primarily serving the Chinese market. As a B2B advertising technology platform it has limited direct consumer abuse exposure, though programmatic ad networks generally carry risk of ad fraud, click fraud, and malvertising. No notable public incidents specific to Tanx have been documented outside China.

Tap Payments.history: Tap Payments was founded around 2013 and is headquartered in Kuwait, with offices across the GCC region including Saudi Arabia, UAE, Bahrain, Qatar, Oman, and Egypt. It serves over 120,000 enterprises across the Middle East and North Africa and has received regulatory approval from Saudi Arabia's SAMA and the UAE Central Bank as a licensed payment gateway.
Tap Payments.summary_abuse: Tap Payments is a regulated payment gateway operating in the GCC region with Central Bank licensing in Saudi Arabia and the UAE. Payment processors can be targeted for fraud such as card testing attacks, but Tap operates within regulatory frameworks that mandate anti-fraud controls. No notable public abuse incidents specific to Tap Payments have been documented.

Tapad.history: Tapad was founded in 2010 in New York City by Are Traasdahl and Dag Liodden, pioneering cross-device identity matching for targeted advertising. Telenor acquired Tapad for $360 million in 2016, and Experian subsequently acquired Tapad from Telenor in November 2020 for approximately $280 million, integrating it into Experian's marketing services division.
Tapad.summary_abuse: Tapad is a cross-device advertising and identity-resolution platform that by nature handles large volumes of user tracking data across devices. It has been cited by privacy researchers as an example of pervasive cross-device tracking with limited user opt-out controls. The platform is not a hosting or consumer-facing service, so direct phishing or fraud hosting abuse is not applicable, but it contributes to the broader ad-tech surveillance ecosystem.

Tapatalk.history: Tapatalk was founded in 2009 by Winter Wong in Santa Monica, California to bring mobile-first experiences to traditional web forums. Over time it grew into an aggregation platform used by over 200,000 forums globally, and expanded through acquisitions of several forum hosting platforms including Network54, Yuku, FreeForums, and others to form Tapatalk Groups.
Tapatalk.summary_abuse: Tapatalk aggregates forum communities and allows user-generated content. Forums hosted through its platform have historically been vectors for spam and phishing links posted by compromised or bot accounts. However, abuse primarily follows the underlying forums rather than Tapatalk itself, and the platform applies standard moderation tools. No major platform-level abuse incidents are publicly documented.

Tapcart.history: Tapcart was founded in 2017 in Los Angeles as a Shopify-native mobile app builder for e-commerce brands. It raised $4 million from Greycroft in 2019, followed by a $50 million Series B led by Left Lane Capital in June 2021 with Shopify as a strategic investor, bringing total funding to over $65 million.
Tapcart.summary_abuse: Tapcart is a mobile commerce app builder tightly integrated with Shopify. It has no notable abuse signal; it requires an active Shopify store to use and is not a consumer-facing free hosting platform. Fake or fraudulent storefronts would need to be addressed at the Shopify level rather than Tapcart itself.

Tapfiliate.history: Founded in 2014 in Amsterdam, Netherlands, Tapfiliate was a bootstrapped affiliate marketing SaaS platform. In 2021, it was acquired by Admitad, a global affiliate marketing network, making it a subsidiary of that company.
Tapfiliate.summary_abuse: Tapfiliate is an affiliate marketing platform used by merchants to run legitimate referral programs. Affiliate marketing platforms can occasionally be misused to set up fraudulent commission schemes, but Tapfiliate's legitimate use base dominates significantly. No notable documented abuse incidents are publicly known.

TapMango.history: TapMango was founded in 2012 in Toronto, Canada as a customizable customer loyalty and online ordering platform primarily serving retail and restaurant businesses. No notable ownership changes or acquisition events have been documented.
TapMango.summary_abuse: TapMango is a B2B loyalty and ordering platform for retailers and restaurants. It has no notable abuse signal as it operates in a controlled merchant onboarding context. The platform does not provide free consumer-facing hosting or anonymous signup, reducing its exposure to phishing or fraud toolkits.

Tapsell.history: Tapsell was founded in 2015 in Iran as a video ad network for mobile apps, growing to become one of Iran's leading digital advertising platforms. It serves the Iranian mobile app ecosystem with targeted ad delivery and is associated with the Cafe Bazaar app store ecosystem.
Tapsell.summary_abuse: Tapsell is an Iranian digital advertising platform focused on the domestic mobile app market. As an ad network it carries standard ad-fraud risks such as click fraud and impression fraud, but no notable external abuse incidents are publicly documented. It primarily serves verified Iranian app developers and is not a consumer-facing hosting service.

Tapstream.history: Tapstream was founded in 2012 in Victoria, Canada by Benjamin Fox and Slaven Radic as a mobile app marketing attribution and analytics platform. The company raised $677K in seed funding in 2013. It appears to have limited recent public activity.
Tapstream.summary_abuse: Tapstream is a mobile marketing attribution and analytics tool for app developers. It has no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B analytics platform with no consumer-facing content hosting surface that would attract phishing or fraud operators.

Taptop.history: Taptop is a SaaS website builder and hosting platform operating at taptop.pro; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.
Taptop.summary_abuse: Taptop is a website builder SaaS that allows users to host sites on its platform; website builders with free or low-cost tiers can be used to host phishing pages, but no specific documented incidents are known for Taptop.

Target2Sell.history: Target2Sell was founded in 2012 in Paris, France as an e-commerce personalization and product recommendation SaaS. It was acquired by French marketplace unicorn Mirakl in April 2022 and integrated into Mirakl's e-commerce platform portfolio.
Target2Sell.summary_abuse: Target2Sell is a B2B e-commerce personalization engine. It has no notable abuse signal; the platform operates in an enterprise SaaS context with no consumer-facing content hosting that would attract phishing or fraud operators.

TargetBay.history: TargetBay is an all-in-one e-commerce marketing platform offering email, SMS, reviews, and loyalty automation. The founding year is not prominently disclosed; it appears to have launched around 2015-2016 as a US-based SaaS for e-commerce merchants.
TargetBay.summary_abuse: TargetBay is a B2B e-commerce marketing automation platform. It has no notable abuse signal; access requires merchant onboarding and it does not offer consumer-facing free content hosting.

Tars.history: Tars was founded in 2016 by Ish Jindal and Vinit Agrawal, with primary development based in Bangalore, India, and US incorporation in Newark, Delaware. The company raised approximately $100K in early-stage funding including support from Upekkha and Hat-Trick Capital. The platform evolved from a no-code chatbot builder to a broader conversational AI platform targeting enterprise customer service and marketing use cases.
Tars.summary_abuse: Tars is a no-code chatbot and conversational AI platform that can be used to create landing page chatbots and website bots. While chatbot platforms can theoretically be used to create convincing phishing conversational flows, Tars targets legitimate marketing and customer service teams. No documented phishing campaigns or fraud incidents specifically leveraging Tars chatbots have been publicly reported.

Tashi.history: Tashi is a web-based booking and reservation management platform for accommodation providers and tour operators. It operates at tashi.travel and targets the travel and hospitality sector. Specific founding details are not publicly documented.
Tashi.summary_abuse: Tashi is a B2B booking software for accommodation providers. It has no notable abuse signal as it is used exclusively by vetted hospitality businesses managing their own reservation systems.

Task Analytics.history: Task Analytics is a SaaS analytics platform that captures customer intent and task completion data on websites. It is designed to measure interaction effectiveness through post-visit surveys. The company appears to be based in the Nordics and has operated since the mid-2010s.
Task Analytics.summary_abuse: Task Analytics is a B2B web analytics and survey tool. It has no notable abuse signal; it operates as a backend analytics service for enterprise clients without consumer-facing hosting features.

Tatari.history: Tatari was founded in 2016 by Philip Inghelbrecht and Mike Swinson in San Francisco as a data-driven TV advertising measurement and buying platform. The company raised venture funding and grew to serve direct-to-consumer brands looking to measure ROI on linear and streaming TV advertising.
Tatari.summary_abuse: Tatari is a B2B TV advertising analytics and buying platform. It has no notable abuse signal; the platform requires enterprise contracts and deals with broadcast advertising inventory, which is not a vector for phishing or fraud toolkits.

Tauros Media.history: Tauros Media is an ecommerce and omni-channel system provider offering integrated retail solutions; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.
Tauros Media.summary_abuse: Tauros Media is an ecommerce system provider with no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B retail platform without documented involvement in phishing or fraud.

Tawk.to.history: Tawk.to was founded in 2013 and rapidly grew to become one of the most widely used free live chat platforms, distinguished by its permanently free core product monetized through chat agent hiring services. It has remained independent without major ownership changes.
Tawk.to.summary_abuse: Tawk.to is a widely-used live chat widget with a permanently free tier. The free tier with no-registration-required usage lowers barriers and could be misused to embed social-engineering chat widgets on phishing sites, though this represents a small fraction of use. No major documented abuse campaigns are publicly associated with Tawk.to.

Taxdome.history: TaxDome was founded in 2017 by brothers Victor and Ilya Radzinsky as a practice management platform for accounting, tax, and bookkeeping firms. It has grown rapidly to serve thousands of accounting firms globally and publishes voluntary annual transparency reports since 2024.
Taxdome.summary_abuse: TaxDome is a practice management platform for accounting and tax firms. It has no notable abuse signal; it serves credentialed professional services firms and handles client tax documents in a controlled B2B environment with no public content hosting features.

TCAdmin.history: TCAdmin is a game server hosting control panel developed around 2007, widely used by game server hosting providers to manage dedicated game servers. No major ownership changes documented.
TCAdmin.summary_abuse: TCAdmin is used by game server hosting providers to manage game servers. Game server hosting can attract some illegitimate use for hosting cheats or exploits, but no documented large-scale abuse pattern specific to TCAdmin is known.

TDesign.history: TDesign is an open-source design system and UI component library launched by Tencent in 2021, offering React, Vue, and mobile components for enterprise applications.
TDesign.summary_abuse: TDesign has no notable abuse signal. It is a UI component library from Tencent with no consumer-facing hosted surface.

TDO Software.history: TDO Software (The Digital Office) was created by endodontist Dr. Gary Carr in San Diego, with Version 1.01 tested in 1998 and commercial launch in early 2000. Dr. Carr developed it as practice management software tailored for endodontic offices, drawing on his research into microscopic endodontics. The product became the most widely used practice management system in endodontics, serving approximately 2,000 practices across 22 countries. In March 2024, Valsoft Corporation acquired TDO Software for approximately $15-16 million.
TDO Software.summary_abuse: TDO Software is a highly specialized dental practice management system for endodontists, handling appointment scheduling, patient records, and clinical documentation. It operates in a regulated healthcare environment and processes patient health information. No consumer-facing surface, no anonymous access, and no documented abuse incidents make this a zero-risk platform from a fraud or phishing perspective.

Teachable.history: Teachable was founded in 2014 by Ankur Nagpal in New York as an online course platform. It was acquired by Hotmart (a Brazilian digital products company) in 2020 for a reported $250 million.
Teachable.summary_abuse: Teachable is a popular online course platform. Like other course-hosting platforms, it has a moderate potential for scam courses or fraudulent digital products, but no documented large-scale specific abuse patterns are known. Hotmart's acquisition brought additional governance.

Teads.history: Teads was founded in 2011 and pioneered the inRead video ad format embedded within publisher article content. Altice acquired Teads in 2017. In February 2025, Outbrain completed its acquisition of Teads for approximately $900 million and subsequently rebranded the combined entity under the Teads name, trading as a NASDAQ-listed public company.
Teads.summary_abuse: Teads is a programmatic advertising platform embedded on major publisher websites. Like all ad-tech platforms it carries risk of malvertising, where malicious third parties inject deceptive or malware-laden creatives through the ad ecosystem. Teads has a published AUP and ad quality team, but as a large-scale programmatic network it is periodically involved in malvertising incidents common to the industry. Its tracker density is high due to the nature of cross-site ad targeting.

Tealium.history: Tealium was founded in 2008 in San Diego and became a leading enterprise tag management and customer data platform. It pioneered the concept of universal tag management (iQ) and later expanded into a customer data hub. No major acquisitions; privately held with significant VC funding.
Tealium.summary_abuse: Tealium is an enterprise tag management and customer data platform. It sits at the center of marketing data flows and is an ad-tech adjacent platform. No specific documented abuse incidents are known, but it handles large volumes of personal data for ad targeting purposes.

Tealium AudienceStream.history: Tealium AudienceStream is a product within the Tealium customer data platform suite. Tealium was founded in 2008 in San Diego.
Tealium AudienceStream.summary_abuse: Tealium AudienceStream is an enterprise product module within Tealium's ad-tech and customer data infrastructure. No specific documented abuse incidents are known.

Tealium Consent Management.history: Tealium Consent Management is a product within the Tealium customer data platform suite. Tealium was founded in 2008 in San Diego.
Tealium Consent Management.summary_abuse: Tealium Consent Management is an enterprise product module within Tealium's ad-tech and customer data infrastructure. No specific documented abuse incidents are known.

TeamBrain.history: TeamBrain is a self-learning AI knowledge management platform for enterprise teams; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.
TeamBrain.summary_abuse: TeamBrain is an internal knowledge management tool with no notable abuse signal; it serves enterprise teams with no consumer-facing surface used for fraud.

TeamCity.history: TeamCity was first released in 2006 by JetBrains, the Czech software company behind IntelliJ IDEA and other developer tools. It has remained a flagship JetBrains product for CI/CD with both on-premises and cloud-hosted deployment options.
TeamCity.summary_abuse: TeamCity has no notable abuse signal as a developer-facing CI/CD server. JetBrains did disclose a critical authentication bypass vulnerability in TeamCity (CVE-2024-23917) in early 2024 that was exploited in the wild by nation-state actors to compromise build pipelines, but this is a security vulnerability in the product rather than platform abuse. The vendor responded with a prompt patch release.

TeamLinkt.history: TeamLinkt is a team management and coordination SaaS for sports teams and organizations. It is a Canadian-founded startup with limited publicly available history.
TeamLinkt.summary_abuse: TeamLinkt is a team coordination platform for sports organizations. It has no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B and consumer team management tool without free anonymous content hosting features.

TeamSystem Commerce.history: TeamSystem Commerce is the e-commerce product line of TeamSystem SpA, an Italian business management software company founded in 1979 and headquartered in Pesaro, Italy. TeamSystem is a significant Italian SMB software vendor that has been backed by private equity including HgCapital.
TeamSystem Commerce.summary_abuse: TeamSystem Commerce is an Italian e-commerce platform for SMBs. It has no notable abuse signal as it operates within the established TeamSystem business software ecosystem serving vetted Italian businesses.

Teamtailor.history: Founded around 2014 in Stockholm, Sweden, Teamtailor is an all-in-one recruitment platform (ATS, career site, and analytics) serving over 10,000 companies in 90+ countries. The company is SOC 2 and ISO 27001/27701 certified, operates under GDPR compliance, and provides AI-powered recruitment tools.
Teamtailor.summary_abuse: Teamtailor is an ATS/recruitment SaaS platform serving HR professionals and businesses. The platform requires business signup with verified company credentials, limiting anonymous abuse. As an applicant tracking system, it handles candidate PII under GDPR protections. The vendor maintains SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certifications with a trust center. No notable abuse incidents are documented.

TeamUnify.history: TeamUnify is a swim team management platform founded in the mid-2000s in the US; it was acquired by Active Network, a sports and events management company.
TeamUnify.summary_abuse: TeamUnify is a niche swim team management platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves sports organizations and coaches with scheduling and registration tools but does not offer content hosting or anonymous access.

TeamUp.history: TeamUp was founded in 2012 by Matt Pegler, Rupert Baker, and Tom Larsen as fitness class management software built by fitness enthusiasts from MIT. The platform grew to serve boutique gyms, studios, and fitness boxes in the UK, US, and Spain. In September 2022, DaySmart Software acquired TeamUp to expand its portfolio of appointment and scheduling software.
TeamUp.summary_abuse: TeamUp is a boutique fitness management SaaS used by gym owners and studios to handle memberships, scheduling, and payments. Its audience is professional fitness operators, and it processes membership and payment data in a regulated environment. No documented fraud or phishing incidents involving TeamUp have been publicly reported.

TeamViewer.history: Monitis was founded in 2007 as an independent network monitoring SaaS; it was acquired by TeamViewer AG (Germany) and continues to operate as the monitoring arm of the TeamViewer product suite under the monitis.com domain.
TeamViewer.summary_abuse: This network and IT monitoring service (Monitis/TeamViewer) has no notable abuse signal. It is a backend infrastructure monitoring tool with no consumer-facing content-hosting surface.

Tebex.history: Tebex was founded around 2011 (originally as Buycraft) as a game monetization platform for Minecraft servers. It expanded into a full gcommerce platform serving major game studios including Rockstar, Take Two Interactive, and Hypixel. The company was acquired by Overwolf and rebranded from Buycraft to Tebex. It now processes billions in transactions annually with offices in the US, UK, and Singapore, offering Merchant of Record services covering payments, tax, fraud, and compliance.
Tebex.summary_abuse: Tebex is a game commerce platform serving major studios like Rockstar and Take Two. It requires account registration and is subject to a detailed Acceptable Use Policy that prohibits gambling, lootboxes, fake donations, and non-game items, with enforcement procedures including store disabling and fund freezing. The platform has an AML and Sanctions Compliance Policy and ISO certification. While game server monetization platforms can be associated with gambling-like mechanics, Tebex actively prohibits such use. No notable public abuse incidents beyond generic gaming commerce risks.

Tebra.history: Kareo was founded in 2004 by Dan Rodrigues as an EHR and billing platform for independent practices. PatientPop was founded separately as a practice marketing and reputation management platform. The two companies merged under the Tebra brand in 2021, creating a unified EHR+, billing, patient experience, and marketing platform backed by over $130M in venture funding. Tebra now serves over 150,000 healthcare providers with 1,000+ employees.
Tebra.summary_abuse: Tebra is a HIPAA-compliant EHR and practice management platform serving credentialed healthcare providers. Patient data is protected under HIPAA regulations. While the platform includes patient-facing features such as online scheduling, patient portals, payment processing, and broadcast messaging, all users are licensed healthcare professionals or their staff, which limits abuse potential. No significant abuse incidents have been publicly documented. The platform's practice-marketing arm builds custom websites for providers, a potential content-hosting surface, but provider credentialing mitigates fraud risk.

TechTarget.history: TechTarget was founded in 1999 in Newton, Massachusetts as a B2B technology media company providing content and lead generation services to IT vendors. It went public on NASDAQ and grew through acquisitions including Computer Weekly (2019) and a major merger with Informa Tech in 2023.
TechTarget.summary_abuse: TechTarget is a B2B technology media and intent-data platform used by enterprise IT vendors for lead generation. It has no significant consumer-facing surface relevant to fraud or phishing. No notable abuse incidents are publicly documented.

TeeChart.history: Steema Software was founded in 1996 in Girona, Spain, as a data visualization company. TeeChart JS, the JavaScript edition of its charting library, was released as a cross-browser HTML5 Canvas charting component. The library is MIT-licensed for non-commercial use with paid commercial subscriptions for support and updates. TeeChart has been adopted by Fortune 500 companies including Toyota, Volkswagen, Dell, and Samsung.
TeeChart.summary_abuse: TeeChart JS is a client-side JavaScript charting library for data visualization. It has no notable abuse signal — it is a charting component with no user-generated content, no file hosting, no authentication system, no custom domain hosting, and no consumer-facing commerce surface that fraudsters target. No documented incidents of TeeChart being used for phishing, scams, or malware delivery exist.

Teeinblue.history: Teeinblue was founded around 2019 as a Shopify app for product personalization and print-on-demand. It is headquartered in Singapore and has grown to support merchants generating over $600 million in revenue through personalized products. The app integrates with 30+ print-on-demand partners and supports Shopify, Etsy, and other e-commerce platforms. No notable ownership changes or controversies.
Teeinblue.summary_abuse: Teeinblue is a Shopify app for product personalization in print-on-demand e-commerce. As a POD personalization tool, it could theoretically be used to create counterfeit or IP-infringing products, but the platform itself does not host content or facilitate anonymous transactions. The company operates as a legitimate Singapore-based business with a published privacy policy and terms of service. No notable public incidents of abuse have been documented.

Tekion.history: Founded in 2016 by Jay Vijayan, former CIO of Tesla, and Guru Sankararaman. Launched its Automotive Retail Cloud (ARC) platform in the first dealership in 2019. Raised over $565 million from investors including Index Ventures, Advent International, and strategic OEM investors GM, Hyundai, and BMW i Ventures, reaching a $4 billion valuation in 2024. Named to the Forbes Cloud 100 in 2023. The platform now powers over 3,000 dealerships with 53% year-over-year growth.
Tekion.summary_abuse: Tekion is an enterprise B2B SaaS platform for automotive retail (DMS, CRM, payments). It hosts no user-generated content, offers no anonymous signup, and has no consumer-facing content surface. The platform is used by car dealerships and OEMs for operational management, with strong security controls including SOC 2, ISO 27001, and PCI DSS compliance, a responsible disclosure program, and incident management procedures. Abuse is effectively nonexistent.

TeleportHQ.history: TeleportHQ was founded around 2018 as a low-code visual website builder platform. The company is headquartered in Romania with a team of designers, developers, and data scientists. It open-sourced its code-generator components on GitHub while maintaining a commercial SaaS visual builder and hosting platform. The platform supports exporting production-ready code across React, Vue, Angular, and other frameworks and integrates with headless CMS platforms and Figma.
TeleportHQ.summary_abuse: TeleportHQ is a low-code website builder platform with a free tier and custom domain hosting on Professional plans. Like other website builders, it could theoretically be used for creating deceptive sites, but there are no notable public incidents of TeleportHQ being widely used for phishing or scams. The platform offers a Report Abuse page, publishes a privacy policy and terms of service, and has community guidelines. The free tier includes watermarked websites on TeleportHQ subdomains, and custom domains require a paid plan, adding some friction for abuse.

Telescope.history: Created by Sacha Greif around 2014 as an open-source Meteor-based social news CMS, similar to Hacker News or Reddit. Later evolved into Telescope Nova, a React+Meteor app platform. The project was eventually succeeded by Vulcan.js. Development has been dormant since the decline of the Meteor ecosystem.
Telescope.summary_abuse: Telescope is an open-source CMS framework with no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted application platform with no consumer-facing hosted service, making it an unlikely target for abuse.

Tempus.history: Tempus was created by darkroomengineering as a lightweight animation frame manager for JavaScript applications. It consolidates multiple requestAnimationFrame loops into a single tick with priority and FPS control. The project is MIT-licensed and has no notable ownership changes or forks.
Tempus.summary_abuse: Tempus has no abuse signal; it is a client-side JavaScript utility library that manages requestAnimationFrame callbacks. It performs no network requests, collects no data, and has no server-side component or consumer-facing surface for fraudsters to exploit.

Tencent Analytics (腾讯分析).history: Launched around 2011 by Tencent as a free web analytics service primarily serving the Chinese market. The service (腾讯分析) was widely adopted by Chinese websites. It was eventually deprecated as Tencent consolidated its analytics and marketing technology offerings, with the domain redirecting away from the standalone service.
Tencent Analytics (腾讯分析).summary_abuse: Tencent Analytics is a free web analytics tracker owned by Tencent, the Chinese mega-corporation. As a client-side JavaScript tracker, it could theoretically be abused for data collection without user consent. However, as a free product from a regulated public company in China, it operates under Chinese data protection and cybersecurity laws. No major documented independent abuse incidents specific to this analytics service are publicly recorded outside of general concerns about Chinese tech company data practices.

Tencent Cloud.history: Launched in September 2013 as Tencent's cloud computing division. Expanded globally from 2016 onward with data centers across Asia, Europe, and the Americas. Created Tencent Cloud and Smart Industries Group (CSIG) in 2018. As of 2024 holds ~2% global cloud market share and 15% in China as the third-largest provider. Operates 65 availability zones across 22 regions with 3200+ CDN nodes.
Tencent Cloud.summary_abuse: Tencent Cloud is a major cloud infrastructure provider with standard abuse reporting and AUP enforcement. Cloud infrastructure is occasionally abused for hosting malicious content, but legitimate use overwhelmingly dominates. A notable 2024 API disruption affected ~1,957 clients. As a Chinese cloud provider, data sovereignty and cross-border data transfer are subject to applicable local laws.

Tencent QQ.history: First released in February 1999 as OICQ by Tencent, inspired by ICQ; renamed QQ after AOL trademark concerns. Launched QQ International in 2009 and WebQQ in 2009. Part of a notable 2010 privacy dispute with Qihoo 360 over alleged user-data scanning. Maintained by Tencent's Platform & Content Group (PCG); reported ~597 million monthly active accounts as of March 2023.
Tencent QQ.summary_abuse: QQ is a mainstream consumer IM platform with no disproportionate abuse profile relative to its ~597M user base. Typical platform abuse (spam, phishing links in chat) is managed through automated detection and account takedown. Notable concerns center on privacy rather than active abuse: EFF's 2015 secure-messaging scorecard gave QQ 2/7 points (no end-to-end encryption, no forward secrecy, closed-source). Some antivirus vendors have flagged QQ as adware or potentially unwanted due to bundled ads and data-collection practices. Tencent cooperates with Chinese government surveillance requirements, enabling keyword monitoring of conversations. A 2010 dispute with Qihoo 360 alleged unauthorized user-data scanning. Tencent operates a security response center with a responsible-disclosure process.

Tencent Waterproof Wall.history: Tencent Waterproof Wall (腾讯防水墙) is a security verification and anti-spam service developed by Tencent Holdings Ltd., one of the world's largest technology conglomerates founded in 1998 in Shenzhen, China. The product operates under the 007.qq.com domain as part of Tencent's cloud security suite. Tencent is publicly traded on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange (0700.HK).
Tencent Waterproof Wall.summary_abuse: Tencent Waterproof Wall is a security/CAPTCHA verification service designed to prevent bots, spam, and automated abuse. As an anti-abuse tool itself, it is not a vector for abuse but rather a mitigation measure. Being operated by Tencent, a publicly traded Chinese mega-corporation, the service benefits from substantial security engineering resources. No notable abuse incidents involving the service itself have been documented.

Tend.history: Tend was founded in 2015 by Ryan and Marty (last names not publicly disclosed) as Tend, LLC based in New Lennox, Illinois. The company offered a conversion analytics SaaS that tracked website visitor behavior and attributed it to marketing channels. A 21-day free trial was offered at $49/month. The product appears to have been discontinued; the tend.io domain now hosts an unrelated personal site as of at least 2024, with the last known product activity around 2022.
Tend.summary_abuse: Tend is a small, now-defunct conversion analytics SaaS with no notable abuse signal. As a JavaScript tracking snippet embedded on client websites, it could theoretically appear on any site including fraudulent ones, but the tool's obscurity, lack of free-tier hosting features, and absence of documented abuse reports make it an unlikely target for systematic abuse. The vendor was a two-person LLC with a published privacy policy but no visible anti-abuse infrastructure or bug bounty program. No public incidents have been reported.

Tendenci.history: Founded by Edward Schipul through his company Schipul / Kinect LLC. Tendenci was initially developed as a proprietary CMS and later released as open source under GPLv3 around 2010. It is a Python/Django-based Association Management Software (AMS) tailored for nonprofits and professional associations. Over 1 million users. Recognized on the Inc. 5000 list. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events.
Tendenci.summary_abuse: Tendenci is an open-source Association Management Software (AMS) offered as a managed SaaS for nonprofits and associations. It has minimal abuse signal — it is a backend platform for managing memberships, events, and content, with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters commonly target. The vendor operates standard hosted security practices on AWS. No notable abuse-related incidents have been publicly documented.

Tender Support.history: Launched in 2009 by ENTP as a customer support platform with public forums, knowledge base, and help desk features. Sister product to Lighthouse issue tracking. The product appears to have seen little active development since around 2016, though the service remains operational.
Tender Support.summary_abuse: Tender Support is a hosted help desk and customer support platform. It facilitates ticket-based support interactions between businesses and their customers. The platform has no consumer-facing hosting surface for anonymous abuse, as accounts require paid subscriptions. No documented history of notable abuse incidents.

Tengine.history: Tengine originated at Taobao (Alibaba Group) as a fork of Nginx with advanced features for large-scale web serving. It was first open-sourced in December 2011 and is actively developed by engineers from Taobao, Ant Group, and Alibaba Cloud. The project remains under Alibaba Group with no ownership changes or forks of significance.
Tengine.summary_abuse: Tengine is a backend web server infrastructure project with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target. It has no notable abuse signal — it is deployed by operators on their own infrastructure and does not offer any user-facing hosting, redirect, or content delivery features that could be directly abused for phishing, scams, or malware distribution.

TerMed.history: TerMed launched around 2023 as a German online medical appointment booking platform. No notable ownership changes, acquisitions, or deprecation events have been recorded since launch.
TerMed.summary_abuse: TerMed is a B2B medical appointment scheduling platform serving German healthcare providers. It has no notable abuse signal — it is a backend booking system with no consumer-facing features (file hosting, URL shortening, custom domains, mass email) that fraudsters typically exploit. As a regulated-healthcare SaaS operating under German/EU law, the service handles sensitive health data, which inherently limits its appeal for abuse.

Terminalfour.history: Terminalfour was founded in 1998 in Dublin, Ireland as a web content management platform focused on the higher education sector. The company has grown to serve over 250 universities and colleges across 32,000+ websites globally, with offices in Ireland, the UK, the US, and Australia. The platform has evolved from a CMS into a full digital engagement suite including email marketing, AI-powered search, and course discovery tools. No major acquisitions or ownership changes are publicly documented.
Terminalfour.summary_abuse: Terminalfour is a web content management platform serving the higher education sector with over 250 university clients. The platform requires enterprise contracts and is used by legitimate academic institutions for managing their public-facing websites and portals. There are no documented reports of abuse involving the platform. As a self-hosted CMS, it has no consumer-facing free tier or anonymous signup capabilities.

Terminus.history: Terminus was founded in 2014 in Atlanta, Georgia as an account-based marketing (ABM) platform. It raised significant venture capital and became a leading ABM vendor. In 2021, it merged with Sigstr and further acquired other ABM tools to expand its platform.
Terminus.summary_abuse: Terminus is a B2B account-based marketing platform used by enterprise sales and marketing teams. It has no significant consumer-facing surface relevant to fraud or phishing. No notable abuse incidents are publicly documented.

Termly.history: Founded in 2017 by David Reynier to provide simple website policy generation tools, later expanding into a full compliance suite including consent management, cookie scanning, and DSAR handling. Termly was acquired by group.one (one.com Group AB) in August 2024, joining a portfolio of web services brands including WP Rocket, Rank Math, Imagify, and one.com. The company serves over 2 million businesses and processes more than 1.7 billion consent banner views per month.
Termly.summary_abuse: Termly is a privacy compliance SaaS platform embedded as a third-party script on customer websites to manage cookie consent and generate legal policies. Its abuse surface is limited but relevant: the free tier allows any website operator to deploy a consent banner with minimal upfront verification, and cookie consent platforms have historically been used to implement dark-pattern consent flows. However, Termly publishes an Acceptable Use Policy, offers abuse reporting channels through its support team, and as a privacy-first compliance company has strong incentives to maintain integrity. No major public incidents of large-scale platform abuse have been documented. The primary abuse risk comes from individual website operators using the tool deceptively, not from platform-level vulnerabilities.

Tern.history: Tern was founded around 2020 by Zoe Rowswell, Kate Walmsley, and Ben Yarwood as Tern Circular LTD in the UK. The company provides a Shopify-integrated circular retail platform for trade-in, buyback, and resale services aimed at fashion and retail brands. No notable ownership changes or acquisitions.
Tern.summary_abuse: Tern has minimal abuse signal. As a Shopify-integrated trade-in and resale platform for retail brands, it operates within the legitimate circular economy space. The platform requires merchant integration and serves established retail brands, which limits abuse potential. No documented abuse incidents.

TerriaJS.history: Originated as NationalMap within CSIRO's Data61 in 2014, evolving into the TerriaJS library in 2015. Spun out as Terria Pty Ltd, an independent Australian company, with CSIRO assigning the intellectual property in 2024. Raised a seed round in 2024 and opened Series A in 2026. No major forks, relicensing events, or controversies.
TerriaJS.summary_abuse: TerriaJS is a geospatial data visualization framework with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target. It enables organizations to build map-based digital twins from their own data. No documented abuse patterns, phishing campaigns, or scam incidents are associated with TerriaJS. The framework has no anonymous-signup or content-hosting features that could be abused by bad actors.

Tessitura.history: Originally developed by the Metropolitan Opera beginning in the mid-1990s and launched in 2000 to replace its disparate ticketing, fundraising, and CRM systems. Spun off as Tessitura Network, a nonprofit 501(c)(3) organization founded by seven major US arts organizations in 2001. Cloud hosting option added in 2021 via AWS and Rackspace. Over 750 licensees across 10 countries as of 2024.
Tessitura.summary_abuse: Tessitura is an enterprise CRM and ticketing platform for nonprofit arts and cultural organizations, operated as a 501(c)(3) nonprofit with over 750 member organizations. The software handles patron PII and payment data but targets a narrow regulated sector with a lifetime license model, has no free tier, and offers no consumer-facing content hosting. PCI DSS Level 1 compliant for its payment processing (Tessitura Merchant Services). Its architecture limits exposure to common consumer-facing abuse patterns. No notable public abuse incidents documented.

Testflow.history: Founded around 2022 by Thorben Hasberg as testflow Systems GmbH in Cologne, Germany. The platform was developed to digitize manual workflows for COVID-19 test centers. No notable ownership changes or deprecation events since.
Testflow.summary_abuse: Testflow is a niche German SaaS platform for test center workflow management with no notable abuse signal. It is a closed back-office system for managing patient check-in, test processing, and result delivery — it offers no consumer-facing content hosting, no anonymous signup, and no custom-domain support. Data is hosted on German Telekom Cloud servers. The market of COVID-19 test centers has contracted significantly since 2023, further limiting the attack surface.

TestFreaks.history: TestFreaks is a Swedish product review aggregation service founded around 2006, providing e-commerce stores with independent review content. It has remained a niche player in the product review space.
TestFreaks.summary_abuse: TestFreaks is a product review platform for e-commerce sites. Review platforms can be subject to fake or incentivized review submission, though TestFreaks markets itself as an impartial aggregator. No significant documented abuse campaigns are associated with TestFreaks.

Testimonial Robot.history: Testimonial Robot is a customer testimonial and review widget platform founded since at least 2012. The service provides an embeddable reviews widget for websites, blogs, and ecommerce stores, with customization via CSS.
Testimonial Robot.summary_abuse: Testimonial Robot is a customer review widget for websites. It requires business registration and provides embeddable testimonials. Review widgets could be used to display fake reviews, but the platform requires legitimate business integration. No notable abuse incidents documented.

Text In Church.history: Text In Church was founded as a church communication SaaS platform, born out of a church plant. The company is bootstrapped and headquartered in Parkville, Missouri. It has grown to serve over 38,000 church leaders. No notable ownership changes or controversies.
Text In Church.summary_abuse: Text In Church is a niche church communication platform focused on text messaging and calling for religious organizations. As a bootstrapped B2B SaaS serving churches, it has minimal abuse signal. The platform offers mass-texting and email capabilities but targets legitimate religious organizations. The vendor publishes a privacy policy, terms of use, and an acceptable use policy. No documented patterns of phishing, spam, or scam hosting are associated with the platform.

Text Marketer.history: Founded in 1999 as one of the first business SMS providers in the UK. Built an in-house web-based SMS platform and API. Grew to serve thousands of businesses from SMEs to large PLCs and over 110 marketing agencies. No notable ownership changes, acquisitions, or deprecation events.
Text Marketer.summary_abuse: Text Marketer is a UK bulk SMS provider. SMS platforms carry inherent abuse risk as they can be used for spam, phishing, and unsolicited marketing. Text Marketer addresses this through a published Acceptable Use Policy explicitly prohibiting spam, phishing, fraud, and unsolicited messaging. The platform requires account creation (not fully anonymous) and serves legitimate UK businesses. As a long-established (since 1999) provider with UK-based support and a self-service signup flow, abuse is managed through policy enforcement rather than proactive technical screening. The SMS channel category overall sees abuse, but Text Marketer's AUP and UK regulatory compliance provide baseline mitigation.

Textalk.history: Textalk AB was founded in 1979 in Mölndal, Sweden. The company started by creating the world's first digital newspaper for the visually impaired in partnership with GöteborgsPosten. It now operates multiple SaaS products including Abicart (ecommerce), Prenly (digital publishing), and Webarch (content accessibility). The company serves major Nordic publishers including Aftonbladet, Dagens Nyheter, and Bonnier News. No notable ownership changes or controversies.
Textalk.summary_abuse: Textalk provides ecommerce and digital publishing SaaS platforms serving established Nordic media and retail companies. Its platforms are B2B with organizational onboarding, limiting abuse surface. As a Swedish company founded in 1979, it operates under EU/GDPR regulation with a known corporate identity. No notable abuse incidents have been documented.

Texthelp.history: Texthelp was founded in 1996 in the UK, developing assistive technology for people with reading and writing difficulties. Key products include Read&Write literacy support software, Equatio math accessibility, and ReachDeck (formerly BrowseAloud) for web accessibility. In the mid-2020s, Texthelp merged with N2y to form Everway, a combined company with 500+ employees and 250M+ users globally, headquartered in Ohio, USA.
Texthelp.summary_abuse: Texthelp (now Everway) develops assistive technology products including literacy support, math accessibility, and web accessibility solutions. These products have no meaningful abuse signal: they are used by schools, universities, and enterprises via sales-gated institutional procurement, with no free-tier hosting, no custom-domain publishing, and no anonymous signup. The company serves institutional customers with standard data protection practices. No notable abuse incidents have been reported.

Textline.history: Textline was founded in 2015 as a business texting platform focused on customer support and operations teams; no notable ownership changes or acquisitions since.
Textline.summary_abuse: Textline is a business texting platform with no notable abuse signal. Its SMS capabilities could theoretically be used for spam, but the platform requires business registration and does not offer anonymous or KYC-free signup, making it an unlikely tool for bad actors relative to unregulated bulk-SMS services.

Textmagic.history: Textmagic was founded in 2001 by Dan Houghton in the United Kingdom as an SMS service. In 2013 a management buyout occurred under Priit Vaikmaa, who had joined in 2007. The company expanded into the US market in 2014 and launched a new platform in 2023, adding email campaign features in 2025.
Textmagic.summary_abuse: Textmagic is a bulk SMS and email marketing platform with documented pay-as-you-go access, creating moderate abuse risk through potential use for SMS spam and phishing campaigns. The platform requires account registration and has terms of service prohibiting spam, but mass-SMS features are inherently dual-use. No notable public incidents of large-scale abuse have been reported.

Textpattern CMS.history: Textpattern CMS was created by Dean Allen in 2003 as a lightweight, tag-based CMS originally meant for personal publishing. It became one of the earliest PHP/MySQL CMSes alongside WordPress and Drupal. Allen released the source code under the GPL. The project has been maintained by a volunteer community since Allen stepped back; it remains niche but still actively maintained as of the mid-2020s.
Textpattern CMS.summary_abuse: Textpattern CMS is a self-hosted open-source CMS with no notable abuse signal. As a self-hosted tool, abuse depends entirely on how individual site operators configure it; the CMS itself has no consumer-facing surface that bad actors specifically target.

TG Track.history: TG Track is a Russian Telegram bot analytics service (tgtrack.ru) that analyses subscription data for Telegram channels; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
TG Track.summary_abuse: TG Track is a Telegram channel analytics tool with no documented abuse signal; its narrow use case for channel owners does not attract phishing or fraud activity.

ThankYou Analytics.history: ThankYou Analytics is a marketing analytics platform focused on data-driven campaign measurement; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.
ThankYou Analytics.summary_abuse: ThankYou Analytics is a marketing analytics tool with no notable abuse signal; it is a B2B measurement platform with no consumer-facing features used for fraud.

Thanx.history: Thanx was founded in 2012 in San Francisco as a customer loyalty and engagement platform targeting restaurants and retail. It raised VC funding and focuses on data-driven loyalty programs for brick-and-mortar businesses; no notable acquisitions or ownership changes are publicly documented.
Thanx.summary_abuse: Thanx is an enterprise loyalty platform used by restaurants and retailers; it captures customer purchase data and spending insights. No notable abuse signal. The platform collects consumer transaction data for personalized marketing, which carries standard privacy considerations but is not associated with fraud or scam infrastructure.

Thawte.history: Thawte was founded in 1995 in South Africa by Mark Shuttleworth (later founder of Ubuntu/Canonical) as one of the first SSL certificate authorities outside the United States. VeriSign acquired Thawte in 1999. VeriSign's SSL business, including Thawte, was subsequently acquired by Symantec in 2010. DigiCert acquired Symantec's website security business including Thawte in 2017, and Thawte continues operating as a DigiCert brand.
Thawte.summary_abuse: Thawte is a certificate authority; SSL certificates issued by it are occasionally observed on phishing and malware sites, as certificate issuance does not validate site legitimacy beyond domain ownership (DV certificates). DigiCert, Thawte's parent, participates in CA/Browser Forum and has revocation processes for abused certificates. The abuse rate relative to legitimate issuance is low.

The AdsLab.history: The AdsLab is an ad targeting and conversion tracking SaaS; no founding date or notable ownership history is publicly documented.

thttpd.summary_abuse: thttpd has no notable abuse signal; it is a minimal web server used in embedded and low-resource deployments with no consumer-facing surface.

Ticimax.history: Ticimax was founded in 2005 in Istanbul, Turkey, as an ecommerce infrastructure platform. The company grew to serve over 30,000 customers including Turkey's largest ecommerce sites. team.blue, the European hosting and digital presence group, acquired a majority stake in Ticimax to expand its ecommerce platform offerings in the Turkish market.
Ticimax.summary_abuse: Ticimax is a Turkish ecommerce SaaS platform serving small to large Turkish merchants. As a storefront creation platform, it carries theoretical risk of hosting fake shops, but its primary market is legitimate Turkish businesses and it operates under Turkish law. No documented phishing campaigns or large-scale fraud incidents specifically linked to Ticimax infrastructure have been publicly reported.

Tickera.history: Tickera is a WordPress plugin for selling event tickets directly from WordPress sites. It is developed by a Croatian team and offers both a free core plugin and premium add-ons.
Tickera.summary_abuse: Tickera is a WordPress ticket-selling plugin. Ticketing systems can be targeted by fake event scams, but Tickera operates as a self-hosted plugin on the operator's own website, placing fraud accountability on the site owner rather than the platform.

Ticketbro.history: Ticketbro is a mobile-focused ticket booking SaaS for events and travel; no notable ownership changes or founding date are publicly documented.
Ticketbro.summary_abuse: Ticketbro is a legitimate ticket booking platform with no documented abuse incidents. Ticketing systems can be targets for scalping bots, but there is no specific known abuse profile for this platform.

Ticketmeo.history: Ticketmeo is an event ticketing SaaS formerly known as Ploxel, rebranded to Ticketmeo to support both live and virtual events.
Ticketmeo.summary_abuse: Ticketmeo is a legitimate event ticketing platform with no documented abuse incidents. Ticket scalping bots are a general risk for ticketing platforms but no specific abuse profile is documented for Ticketmeo.

TicketSpice.history: TicketSpice is an online event ticketing platform operated by Webconnex, based in Sacramento, California. It has grown to serve thousands of event organizers and is known for transparent pay-per-attendee pricing.
TicketSpice.summary_abuse: TicketSpice is an event ticketing platform. Like all ticketing platforms it can be used to create fraudulent fake events to collect payments, but Webconnex has anti-fraud controls and account review processes. No specific major abuse incidents are publicly documented.

Ticketure.history: Ticketure is a timed-entry ticketing system for cultural and arts organizations, now operated under TixTrack, a specialist ticketing company serving museums, attractions, and theaters.
Ticketure.summary_abuse: Ticketure is a B2B ticketing platform for cultural venues with no notable abuse signal. It operates in the institutional arts and culture sector and does not offer public-facing free tiers that would attract fraudulent activity.

Tictail.history: Tictail was founded in 2012 in Stockholm, Sweden as a free e-commerce platform for small creative sellers. Shopify acquired Tictail in 2018 and subsequently shut down the platform, migrating sellers to Shopify.
Tictail.summary_abuse: Tictail was a now-defunct e-commerce platform acquired and shut down by Shopify in 2018 with no notable abuse signal specific to its operations.

TiddlyWiki.history: TiddlyWiki was created by Jeremy Ruston in 2004 as a unique single-file wiki that runs entirely in the browser without a server. It pioneered the concept of a self-contained notebook application as a single HTML file. A major rewrite (TiddlyWiki 5) was released in 2013. The project has been maintained by Ruston and a community ever since.
TiddlyWiki.summary_abuse: TiddlyWiki is a self-contained open-source notebook application with no notable abuse signal; it has no hosted service or consumer-facing surface.

Tidio.history: Tidio was founded in 2014 in Poland as a live chat and chatbot platform. It raised venture capital funding and expanded to include AI-powered customer service automation, growing to serve over 300,000 businesses worldwide.
Tidio.summary_abuse: Tidio is a live chat and chatbot platform primarily used by legitimate e-commerce and support teams. Its free tier and widget embedding capability could theoretically be misused on phishing pages, but no documented abuse campaigns are associated with Tidio specifically.

TIEIT.history: TIEIT is an AI-powered all-in-one sales and marketing platform; no founding date or ownership history is publicly documented.
TIEIT.summary_abuse: TIEIT is an AI-powered sales and marketing platform with no notable abuse signal; it is a typical SMB-focused CRM tool without documented fraud involvement.

Tiendanube.history: Tiendanube was founded in 2011 in Buenos Aires, Argentina as a SaaS e-commerce platform focused on Latin America. It raised significant VC funding and became one of the leading e-commerce platforms in the region. It also operates as NuvemShop in Brazil.
Tiendanube.summary_abuse: Tiendanube is a major Latin American e-commerce platform. Like any store creation platform, it could theoretically be used to create fraudulent storefronts, but it enforces terms of service and has anti-fraud measures typical of established e-commerce providers.

Tiendy.history: Tiendy is a small e-commerce SaaS for creating virtual shops; no notable founding date or ownership events are publicly documented.
Tiendy.summary_abuse: Tiendy is an e-commerce virtual shop builder. Platforms that allow users to create online storefronts can be used to set up fraudulent stores, but no specific abuse incidents are documented for Tiendy.

Free static hosting with custom domains is a common pattern for hosting malicious landing pages. The platform's small team may have limited abuse detection capacity.

Tiki Wiki CMS Groupware.history: Tiki Wiki CMS Groupware was created in 2002 as a free, open-source PHP-based wiki and CMS platform supporting blogs, forums, wikis, and issue trackers. It is maintained by a volunteer community with no major corporate backing or ownership changes.
