TikShop.history: TikShop is a Shopify app that connects TikTok accounts to Shopify stores, enabling shoppable social feeds. It appears to be a recent integration tool for e-commerce merchants wanting TikTok social commerce.
TikShop.summary_abuse: TikShop is a Shopify integration app for TikTok social commerce. As a connector between Shopify and TikTok, it could be used with fraudulent storefronts, though no specific documented abuse campaigns are attributed to TikShop directly.

Tiktak Pro.history: Tiktak Pro is a Tunisian all-in-one e-commerce platform; no notable founding history or public information is readily available.
Tiktak Pro.summary_abuse: Tiktak Pro is a small regional e-commerce platform from Tunisia with no notable documented abuse signal.

TikTok Pixel.history: TikTok Pixel is the web analytics and conversion tracking pixel for TikTok Ads, developed by ByteDance and launched alongside TikTok's advertising platform around 2019. It is analogous to the Facebook Pixel. TikTok and ByteDance have faced significant regulatory scrutiny in multiple countries regarding data privacy and Chinese government data access.
TikTok Pixel.summary_abuse: TikTok Pixel is an advertising tracking pixel owned by ByteDance (China). It collects extensive behavioural data about web visitors and is subject to significant privacy regulatory concerns in the EU, US, and other jurisdictions regarding data transfers to China. Multiple EU supervisory authorities have issued guidance or bans on its use on government sites. No specific fraud infrastructure abuse is documented, but the data privacy risk profile is high.

Tilda.history: Tilda was founded in 2014 in Russia by Nikita Obuhov as a visual website and landing page builder. It has grown to serve millions of users globally and is particularly popular in Russia and Eastern Europe. No notable acquisitions.
Tilda.summary_abuse: Tilda is a popular page builder platform with a free tier. Page builders offering free hosting are commonly observed in phishing kit deployments because they allow creating convincing landing pages without cost. Tilda's large user base and Russian origin (with associated regulatory considerations) add to this risk profile, though the platform itself enforces terms of service.

Tiled.history: Tiled is a microapp platform for creating interactive sales and marketing content. It targets enterprise B2B sales teams to improve engagement with prospects. Limited founding history is publicly available.

Tiledesk.history: Tiledesk is an open-source live chat and chatbot platform created in Italy, released around 2017. It offers both a cloud-hosted service and a self-hosted option, and has grown as an open-source alternative to commercial live chat platforms.
Tiledesk.summary_abuse: Tiledesk is an open-source live chat platform with self-hosting capability. Its primary use is for legitimate customer support. No documented abuse campaigns are publicly associated with Tiledesk.

Tiller.history: Tiller (originally TillerHQ) was founded in Seattle, Washington, around 2016 by Peter Polson with backing from a private group of Seattle-area technology investors. The company built a spreadsheet-first personal finance tool that automatically imports bank and credit card transactions into Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel, distinguishing itself from app-based budgeting tools by keeping financial data in user-controlled spreadsheets.
Tiller.summary_abuse: Tiller is a personal finance SaaS that connects to bank accounts and imports transactions into spreadsheets. As a financial account aggregation service, it handles sensitive banking credentials and transaction data. No documented fraud incidents or phishing campaigns specifically involving Tiller infrastructure have been publicly reported, but users should be aware of credential aggregation risks.

Tokeet.summary_abuse: Tokeet is a vacation rental property management SaaS used by professional hosts and property managers. It raised significant funding and serves tens of millions of users globally across retail and institutional finance.
TradingView.summary_abuse: TradingView has a modest abuse signal because scammers occasionally embed its public chart widgets in fake broker or investment fraud websites to give them a veneer of legitimacy. TradingView itself is not a broker and does not hold client funds, so direct financial fraud through the platform is limited. Fake investment scheme sites may copy TradingView branding or embed its charts. The platform publishes community guidelines and terms of service but has limited control over how third parties use its embeddable widgets.

TrafficGuard.summary_abuse: TrafficGuard is an ad fraud detection and prevention tool. Its own abuse signal is very low - it is a defensive security product specifically designed to combat click fraud and invalid traffic. It has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target directly.

TrafficStars.history: TrafficStars is an ad network founded around 2014, headquartered in Cyprus, that specializes in adult advertising traffic. It operates as a self-serve advertising platform primarily for adult content publishers and advertisers.
TrafficStars.summary_abuse: TrafficStars is an adult-focused ad network with an elevated abuse signal. Adult ad networks are routinely exploited for malvertising campaigns that redirect users to phishing pages, fake tech-support scams, and malware droppers. TrafficStars has low KYC requirements for advertisers, which makes it attractive for bad actors seeking to buy traffic for scam landing pages. Its traffic quality and advertiser vetting have been flagged by security researchers in the past. Legitimate publishers and advertisers use it, but the adult ad tech sector has a disproportionately high rate of malicious ad injection.

Tramoce.summary_abuse: Tramoce is a cookie-consent compliance widget with no notable abuse signal; it embeds a consent banner on websites and has no content-hosting or anonymous-signup surface that attracts fraudulent use.

Transax.summary_abuse: Transax is a payment gateway used by automotive dealerships with no notable abuse signal; as a payments platform it operates under PCI-DSS requirements, limiting its attractiveness for fraudulent use.

Transcend.summary_abuse: Transcend has no notable abuse signal. It is a data privacy compliance SaaS used by enterprises to manage GDPR and CCPA obligations. The platform helps reduce data exposure rather than enabling it, and does not offer file hosting or consumer-facing open signup.

Transcy.summary_abuse: Transcy is a Shopify store translation app with no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B tool for e-commerce store localization with no consumer-facing content that fraudsters would target.

Transifex.summary_abuse: Transifex has no notable abuse signal. It is a B2B localization SaaS platform used by software teams and enterprises for translation workflows. It does not offer consumer-facing hosting or file storage features that would attract abuse.

TRUENDO.summary_abuse: TRUENDO is a GDPR consent management platform. It is specifically designed to prevent privacy violations and has no documented abuse signal. CMPs are rarely misused.

Trumba.summary_abuse: Trumba is an event calendar SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It serves organizational publishing needs and lacks the anonymous signup or mass-communication features that would attract fraudsters.

Trumpia.history: Trumpia was founded around 2007 in California as a multi-channel marketing platform specializing in SMS, email, social media, and push notifications. It has remained an independent company focused on small and medium business marketing.
Trumpia.summary_abuse: Trumpia is an SMS and multi-channel marketing platform. Mass SMS marketing platforms carry inherent spam risk, as they can be used to send unsolicited text messages at scale. While Trumpia targets legitimate business use, such platforms are sometimes abused for bulk spam campaigns. The vendor maintains standard compliance postures for marketing platforms including TCPA guidelines, which moderates but does not eliminate the risk.

Trunkrs.history: Trunkrs is a Dutch parcel delivery service founded around 2015, operating primarily in the Netherlands. It provides same-day and next-day delivery services to e-commerce businesses and integrates with major e-commerce platforms.
Trunkrs.summary_abuse: Trunkrs is a parcel delivery carrier operating in the Netherlands. It has no notable abuse signal as a logistics service provider, and fraudsters do not typically target shipping carrier tracking widgets for their own purposes.

TrustArc.history: TrustArc (originally TRUSTe) was founded in 1997 as one of the first web privacy certification organizations. It rebranded from TRUSTe to TrustArc in 2017 to reflect its expansion from privacy certifications to a broader privacy management platform. It has remained a leading privacy compliance vendor for enterprises.
TrustArc.summary_abuse: TrustArc is a privacy compliance and consent management platform used by enterprises to satisfy GDPR, CCPA, and other privacy regulations. It is a backend compliance tool with no consumer-facing attack surface relevant to fraud or phishing. No documented abuse incidents are publicly associated with TrustArc.

Trusted Shops.history: Trusted Shops was founded in 1999 in Cologne, Germany as an e-commerce trust certification and buyer protection scheme. It has become one of the leading trust seal providers for online retailers in the German-speaking and European market.
Trusted Shops.summary_abuse: Trusted Shops is a well-established e-commerce trust certification body. Its badges and seals could potentially be copied and placed on fraudulent sites, but the platform itself enforces vetting of merchants. No notable platform-level abuse is recorded.

Trustify.history: Trustify is a review management SaaS that collects and displays customer reviews for businesses. Limited public history is available; it appears to be a small independent product with no notable acquisitions or rebrands.
Trustify.summary_abuse: Trustify is a customer review collection platform with no notable abuse signal. Review platforms can be gamed with fake reviews, but that is a misuse of the content rather than the platform infrastructure being exploited for fraud.

Trustindex.history: Trustindex is a Hungarian review management and aggregation platform founded around 2016. It aggregates reviews from Google, Facebook, and other sources and provides embeddable widgets for websites.
Trustindex.summary_abuse: Trustindex is a review aggregation and widget platform with no notable abuse signal. Review platforms can be gamed with fake reviews by unethical businesses, but the platform infrastructure itself is not a primary fraud vector.

Trustio.history: Trustio is a Swedish customer review platform focused on Nordic e-retailers. It operates as a regional alternative to Trustpilot with limited public history beyond its founding in the mid-2010s.
Trustio.summary_abuse: Trustio is a regional review platform for Swedish e-commerce with no notable abuse signal. It operates in a narrow market and lacks the scale to be a significant fraud vector.

TrustLock.history: TrustLock is a small conversion-optimization SaaS offering trust badges and social proof widgets for e-commerce websites. Founded in the late 2010s, it remains a minor independent product with limited public history.
TrustLock.summary_abuse: TrustLock provides trust badges and social proof elements for websites. These types of tools can be misused on fraudulent storefronts to lend fake credibility, though TrustLock itself is a minor platform with low visibility. The risk is primarily that its badges could be copied or spoofed rather than the platform itself being abused.

Trustmaker.history: Trustmaker is a small social proof and FOMO marketing tool offering customizable notification widgets for websites. Limited public history is available; it appears to be a minor independent SaaS product.
Trustmaker.summary_abuse: Trustmaker provides social proof and FOMO notification widgets. These tools carry moderate risk when deployed on fraudulent storefronts to create artificial urgency. The platform itself is minor and not a known fraud infrastructure component.

Trustpilot.history: Trustpilot was founded in 2007 in Copenhagen, Denmark by Peter Holten Muhlmann. It grew into the world's largest consumer review platform, going public on the London Stock Exchange in 2021. The company has faced repeated controversies over fake reviews and manipulation by businesses paying to remove negative reviews.
Trustpilot.summary_abuse: Trustpilot has a well-documented fake review problem. Businesses pay third-party services to post fake positive reviews or flag legitimate negative reviews for removal. The platform's free tier allows any business to claim a profile, and the open review system is frequently gamed. Trustpilot has been investigated by the UK Competition and Markets Authority and European consumer authorities over fake review practices. It also embeds tracking scripts on merchant sites that collect user behavioral data.

TrustPulse.history: TrustPulse is a social proof and FOMO marketing notification tool launched by Awesome Motive around 2018. Awesome Motive also owns WPForms, OptinMonster, and other WordPress-ecosystem plugins.
TrustPulse.summary_abuse: TrustPulse displays social proof notifications (such as recent purchases) on websites to increase conversions. These widgets could be abused on fake storefronts to create false urgency, but TrustPulse's legitimate use base dominates. No specific documented abuse incidents are publicly attributed to TrustPulse.

Trustspot.history: TrustSpot is a customer reviews and testimonials platform for e-commerce, founded around 2013 in the US. It provides tools for collecting and displaying product and seller reviews.
Trustspot.summary_abuse: TrustSpot is a customer reviews platform for e-commerce stores. Review platforms can be subject to fake or incentivized review submission, though TrustSpot is used primarily by legitimate merchants. No significant documented abuse is associated with TrustSpot.

Trustvox.history: Trustvox is a Brazilian review collection and verification platform for e-commerce, operating primarily in the Brazilian market. It focuses on verifying that reviews come from actual purchasers. Limited public history is available.
Trustvox.summary_abuse: Trustvox is a verified purchase review platform with no notable abuse signal. Its verification model reduces but does not eliminate fake review risk in the Brazilian market.

TrustYou.history: TrustYou was founded in 2008 in Munich, Germany, providing guest feedback and reputation management software to the hospitality industry. The company aggregates hotel reviews from major travel platforms and offers analytics dashboards for hotel operators.
TrustYou.summary_abuse: TrustYou is a hotel reputation management platform with no notable abuse signal. It operates in a B2B hospitality niche and does not provide services that would be exploited by fraudsters.

TruValidate.history: TruValidate is a fraud and identity verification product suite from TransUnion, the major US credit bureau. It provides device intelligence, fraud scoring, and identity verification for financial institutions and fintechs. TransUnion itself was founded in 1968.
TruValidate.summary_abuse: TruValidate is TransUnion's fraud prevention and identity verification platform. As an anti-fraud tool serving financial institutions, it has no notable abuse signal itself. It is used to detect and prevent fraud rather than enable it. TransUnion as a credit bureau is subject to strict regulatory oversight.

TryNow.history: TryNow was founded around 2019 and offers a try-before-you-buy solution for Shopify e-commerce stores, enabling customers to order and pay only for items they keep. It is an independent startup with no notable acquisitions.
TryNow.summary_abuse: TryNow is an e-commerce try-before-you-buy platform with no notable abuse signal. It operates within the Shopify ecosystem and does not expose features that would be attractive to fraudsters.

Tryzens.history: Tryzens was founded in 2004 as a digital commerce consultancy specialising in Salesforce Commerce Cloud implementations. In August 2023 the company underwent a management buyout backed by WestBridge Capital. In 2024 Tryzens expanded its Shopify practice by acquiring Juno, a Shopify Plus specialist.
Tryzens.summary_abuse: Tryzens has no notable abuse signal. It is a digital commerce consultancy providing implementation and managed services, and does not operate consumer-facing infrastructure with open signup or file hosting that would attract fraudulent use.

TSA Commerce.history: TSA Commerce is a WordPress Turbo SEO Affiliate Commerce plugin for integrating affiliate product feeds into WordPress sites; no founding date or notable ownership history is documented.
TSA Commerce.summary_abuse: TSA Commerce is a WordPress affiliate commerce plugin with no notable abuse signal; it is a niche SEO tool with no consumer-facing surface used for phishing or fraud.

TuCalendi.history: TuCalendi is a Spanish online appointment scheduling tool founded around 2018. It targets small businesses and freelancers needing booking and calendar management. Limited public history is available.
TuCalendi.summary_abuse: TuCalendi is an appointment scheduling platform with no notable abuse signal. Scheduling tools are occasionally used to set up fake meeting links as phishing vectors, but TuCalendi is a minor platform with no documented abuse patterns.

Tula.history: Tula is a web-based yoga and fitness studio management software offering payment processing, membership management, and class scheduling for small wellness businesses. It remains a niche independent product.
Tula.summary_abuse: Tula is a yoga studio management SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It serves a narrow vertical and has no consumer-facing surface attractive to fraudsters.

Tulo.history: Tulo is a Swedish subscription management and reader revenue platform, primarily targeting media publishers seeking to implement paywalls and recurring billing. Founded around 2013, it serves Nordic media organizations.
Tulo.summary_abuse: Tulo is a subscription management platform for media publishers with no notable abuse signal. It operates in a B2B media niche and lacks consumer-facing features that would attract fraudsters.

Tumblr.history: Tumblr was founded in 2007 by David Karp. Yahoo acquired it in 2013 for approximately $1.1 billion, later writing off most of its value. After Yahoo was absorbed by Verizon in 2017, Verizon sold Tumblr to Automattic (the company behind WordPress.com) in 2019 for less than $3 million.
Tumblr.summary_abuse: Tumblr has a moderate abuse signal, primarily around spam blogs, bot accounts, and occasionally adult content policy circumvention since its 2018 adult content ban. The platform offers free blog hosting under custom subdomains, free signup without strong KYC, and a history of hosting spam. Automattic has improved moderation since the acquisition but the platform's openness means scam and spam blogs occasionally surface. Abuse is a modest fraction of overall use, which is dominated by legitimate creative and fan communities.

Tunosite.history: Tunosite is a web app builder for startups and businesses combining content management, membership, and e-commerce features. Limited public history is available; it appears to be a minor independent SaaS product.
Tunosite.summary_abuse: Tunosite is a website and app builder with no notable documented abuse. Website builders offering free tiers with custom subdomains can be used to host phishing or scam pages, but Tunosite is obscure enough that it is not a documented fraud vector.

TuoTempo.history: TuoTempo is an Italian patient CRM and healthcare appointment management platform founded around 2012. It serves private healthcare providers in Italy and other European countries with patient communication and scheduling tools.
TuoTempo.summary_abuse: TuoTempo is a healthcare CRM platform with no notable abuse signal. It handles patient data and operates under GDPR in European healthcare contexts, with no consumer-facing features attractive to fraudsters.

Turbo.history: Turbo is the JavaScript framework at the heart of Hotwire, introduced by Basecamp (now 37signals) in 2021. It evolved from Turbolinks, replacing it with a more comprehensive set of tools for building fast web applications without heavy JavaScript frameworks. It is MIT-licensed and open source.
Turbo.summary_abuse: Turbo (Hotwire) is a JavaScript framework for server-rendered web applications. It has no abuse signal; it is a client-side navigation library with no consumer-facing infrastructure that fraudsters would target.

Turbolinks.history: Turbolinks was introduced by DHH at Basecamp around 2012 as a Rails gem to speed up page navigations. It was included by default in Rails applications for years. In 2021 Basecamp released Hotwire with Turbo as its successor, effectively deprecating Turbolinks.
Turbolinks.summary_abuse: Turbolinks is a deprecated JavaScript navigation library for Rails applications. It has no abuse signal.

Turbopack.history: Turbopack was announced by Vercel in October 2022 at Next.js Conf as a Rust-based incremental bundler designed to replace webpack in the JavaScript ecosystem. It is developed under the Turbo monorepo alongside Turborepo and is MIT-licensed.
Turbopack.summary_abuse: Turbopack is a JavaScript/TypeScript build tool with no abuse signal. It is a build-time toolchain with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

TurfJS.history: Turf.js is an open-source geospatial analysis library for JavaScript, created by MapBox around 2014. It provides modular GIS operations usable in browsers and Node.js, covering operations like buffering, clustering, and interpolation. It is now maintained as a community project.
TurfJS.summary_abuse: Turf.js is a geospatial JavaScript library with no abuse signal. It is a data processing utility with no consumer-facing infrastructure.

TuriTop.history: TuriTop is a Spanish booking and reservation software for tourism operators, tour guides, and activity providers, founded around 2014. It focuses on the European tourism market and integrates with payment processors and distribution channels.
TuriTop.summary_abuse: TuriTop is a tour and activity booking platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves a specialized tourism niche with no anonymous or mass-communication features.

Tutor LMS.history: Tutor LMS is a WordPress plugin-based learning management system developed by Themeum, a Bangladeshi WordPress product company. It was released in 2018 and has grown to become one of the popular WordPress LMS plugins, offering both free and premium tiers.
Tutor LMS.summary_abuse: Tutor LMS is a WordPress LMS plugin with no notable abuse signal. It is a self-hosted educational platform without anonymous signup or mass communication features that would attract fraudsters.

TutorCruncher.history: TutorCruncher is a UK-based tutoring agency management platform founded around 2013. It helps tutoring companies manage scheduling, invoicing, and communications. It remains an independent company focused on the educational services vertical.
TutorCruncher.summary_abuse: TutorCruncher is a tutoring management SaaS with no notable abuse signal. It serves a niche educational business vertical with no anonymous access or mass-communication features.

TVSquared.history: TVSquared was founded in 2012 in Edinburgh, Scotland by Calum Smeaton as a cross-platform TV ad measurement and attribution platform. The company raised $34.5 million over 8 rounds. In March 2022, it was acquired by US-listed connected-TV ad platform Innovid for $160 million in cash and stock.
TVSquared.summary_abuse: TVSquared is a TV ad measurement and attribution platform. Like all ad-tech it collects behavioral data at scale for targeting, but no specific fraud or malware abuse incidents are documented.

Twenty Eleven.history: Twenty Eleven was the default WordPress theme for 2011, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Eleven.summary_abuse: Twenty Eleven is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Fifteen.history: Twenty Fifteen was the default WordPress theme for 2015, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Fifteen.summary_abuse: Twenty Fifteen is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Fourteen.history: Twenty Fourteen was the default WordPress theme for 2014, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Fourteen.summary_abuse: Twenty Fourteen is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Nineteen.history: Twenty Nineteen was the default WordPress theme for 2019, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Nineteen.summary_abuse: Twenty Nineteen is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Seventeen.history: Twenty Seventeen was the default WordPress theme for 2017, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Seventeen.summary_abuse: Twenty Seventeen is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Sixteen.summary_abuse: Twenty Sixteen is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Ten.history: Twenty Ten was the default WordPress theme for 2010, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Ten.summary_abuse: Twenty Ten is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Thirteen.summary_abuse: Twenty Thirteen is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Twelve.history: Twenty Twelve was the default WordPress theme for 2012, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Twelve.summary_abuse: Twenty Twelve is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Twenty.history: Twenty Twenty was the default WordPress theme for 2020, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Twenty.summary_abuse: Twenty Twenty is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Twenty-Five.history: Twenty Twenty-Five was the default WordPress theme for 2025, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Twenty-Five.summary_abuse: Twenty Twenty-Five is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Twenty-Four.history: Twenty Twenty-Four was the default WordPress theme for 2024, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Twenty-Four.summary_abuse: Twenty Twenty-Four is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Twenty-One.history: Twenty Twenty-One was the default WordPress theme for 2021, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Twenty-One.summary_abuse: Twenty Twenty-One is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Twenty-Three.history: Twenty Twenty-Three was the default WordPress theme for 2023, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Twenty-Three.summary_abuse: Twenty Twenty-Three is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twenty Twenty-Two.history: Twenty Twenty-Two was the default WordPress theme for 2022, created by the WordPress core team. It is distributed under the GPL and bundled with every WordPress installation.
Twenty Twenty-Two.summary_abuse: Twenty Twenty-Two is a default WordPress bundled theme with no abuse signal.

Twiago.history: Twiago is a German digital advertising platform offering programmatic and transparent display advertising. Limited public history is available; it appears to serve the German-speaking market.
Twiago.summary_abuse: Twiago is an advertising technology platform. Ad tech inherently deploys third-party tracking scripts with higher tracker density, but Twiago is a minor regional player with no documented abuse beyond standard programmatic advertising data collection.

TwicPics.history: TwicPics is a French on-demand image transformation and delivery service founded around 2018. It offers a CDN-backed image processing API that automatically resizes, compresses, and formats images for web delivery.
TwicPics.summary_abuse: TwicPics is an image optimization CDN with no notable abuse signal. It serves technical asset delivery and has no anonymous or mass-communication features that would attract fraudsters.

Twik.history: Twik is an automated personalization and business intelligence platform, likely founded in Israel around 2017. It targets e-commerce stores with automatic content personalization without requiring manual configuration.
Twik.summary_abuse: Twik is a website personalization automation tool for e-commerce. It has no significant consumer-facing surface relevant to fraud or phishing. No documented abuse incidents are publicly associated with Twik.

TWiki.history: TWiki was created by Peter Thoeny in 1998 as one of the earliest structured wikis. It was widely used in enterprise intranets through the 2000s. In 2008 many contributors forked it as Foswiki due to disagreements over governance and trademark, which significantly reduced TWiki's user base.
TWiki.summary_abuse: TWiki has no notable abuse signal specific to its platform. It is a self-hosted enterprise wiki and the major security risk is outdated, unpatched installations on intranet servers.

Twikoo.history: Twikoo is an open-source comment system for static websites built on serverless cloud functions, created by a Chinese developer around 2020. It is deployed on platforms like Tencent CloudBase and Vercel, offering a privacy-friendly alternative to Disqus.
Twikoo.summary_abuse: Twikoo is a self-hosted comment system with no notable abuse signal. It is deployed by individual developers on their own infrastructure and lacks the centralized hosting that would make it a fraud vector.

Twilight CMS.history: Twilight CMS is an obscure content management system with minimal public documentation. No meaningful history, ownership, or notable events are available.
Twilight CMS.summary_abuse: Twilight CMS is an obscure CMS product with no notable abuse signal.

Twilio Authy.history: Twilio Authy was founded in 2012 by Daniel Palacio and acquired by Twilio in 2015. It provided two-factor authentication services via apps, SMS, and voice. In 2024 Twilio announced the shutdown of the Authy desktop apps and migration to mobile-only, signaling a gradual deprecation in favor of Twilio's broader Verify product.
Twilio Authy.summary_abuse: Twilio Authy is a 2FA authentication service with minimal abuse signal. Its purpose is to prevent account takeover rather than enable it. Some SIM-swapping attacks have targeted Authy-protected accounts at the carrier level, but the platform itself is not a fraud tool. Twilio (Authy's parent) was involved in a 2022 phishing breach that exposed some Authy user data, which is notable but was a targeted attack rather than systemic platform abuse.

Twingle.history: Twingle is a German fundraising platform for nonprofits and charitable campaigns, founded around 2013. It enables organizations to create donation pages and processes online donations for German civil society organizations.
Twingle.summary_abuse: Twingle is a fundraising platform for nonprofits. Donation platforms carry some risk of fraudulent charity campaigns, but Twingle serves German-registered nonprofits and requires organizational verification, which limits abuse.

TwistedWeb.history: Twisted Web is the web server component of the Twisted networking framework for Python, which was initially released in 2002. Twisted is one of the oldest asynchronous Python frameworks and is maintained by the Twisted Matrix Laboratories community.
TwistedWeb.summary_abuse: Twisted Web is a Python web server component with no abuse signal. It is a backend server library used in technical Python applications.

TwistPHP.history: TwistPHP is a small open-source PHP web framework. It is a minor community project with no commercial backing and limited public presence.
TwistPHP.summary_abuse: TwistPHP is an obscure PHP web framework with no abuse signal.

Twitch Player.history: Twitch was founded in 2011 as a spin-off from Justin.tv, focusing on gaming live streams. Amazon acquired Twitch in 2014 for approximately $970 million. The Twitch Player is the embeddable video player used to embed Twitch streams on third-party websites.
Twitch Player.summary_abuse: Twitch embeds third-party tracking and advertising scripts with high density. The platform has faced issues with hate raids, ban evasion, and CSAM reports. Its embeddable player carries Twitch's full tracker payload. No significant platform-level fraud vector exists for the embed player specifically, but the broader Twitch platform has documented abuse of its chat and follow systems.

Twitter.history: Twitter was founded in 2006 by Jack Dorsey, Biz Stone, Evan Williams, and Noah Glass. It became a dominant global social network and was acquired by Elon Musk in October 2022 for $44 billion. Following acquisition, Musk rebranded the platform to X, gutted its trust and safety team, and reinstated thousands of banned accounts. The company's legal entity became X Corp.
Twitter.summary_abuse: Twitter/X is extensively abused for phishing, cryptocurrency scams, coordinated inauthentic behavior, spam, and state-sponsored influence operations. Since the 2022 acquisition, the trust and safety team was dramatically reduced, abuse reporting responsiveness declined, and many bad actors had accounts reinstated. The platform's blue-check verification was replaced with a paid subscription, undermining trust signals. Twitter embeds carry heavy tracking payloads including cross-site tracking pixels.

Twitter Ads.history: Twitter Ads launched around 2010-2013 as the advertising platform for Twitter (now X). Following Elon Musk's acquisition of Twitter for $44 billion in October 2022, the platform was rebranded to X in 2023. Twitter Ads continues to operate under X Corp, though advertiser confidence dropped significantly after the acquisition.
Twitter Ads.summary_abuse: Twitter Ads is used by advertisers of all sizes and carries a moderate abuse signal. Fraudulent advertisers have run scam campaigns through the platform, including cryptocurrency scams and impersonation of verified accounts, particularly following weakening of verification controls after the 2022 acquisition. X Corp's moderation posture has been criticised as less responsive than its previous Twitter incarnation, which elevates abuse risk for ad buyers and end users who may encounter scam ads.

Twitter Analytics.history: Twitter Analytics launched around 2012 as a free analytics dashboard for Twitter accounts. It provides tweet performance metrics and audience insights. It remains part of the X/Twitter platform under Elon Musk's ownership.
Twitter Analytics.summary_abuse: Twitter Analytics involves loading Twitter's tracking infrastructure on sites that integrate it. It inherits the platform's broader abuse issues around cross-site tracking and data sharing with X Corp's ad network. The analytics script itself is not a distinct abuse vector beyond the Twitter/X platform generally.

Twitter Emoji (Twemoji).history: Twemoji was released by Twitter in 2014 as an open-source set of emoji images and a JavaScript library for rendering them consistently across platforms. It is licensed under MIT (code) and CC BY 4.0 (graphics) and is widely used across web applications.
Twitter Emoji (Twemoji).summary_abuse: Twemoji is an open-source emoji library with no abuse signal. It is a static asset library used for consistent emoji rendering.

Twitter Flight.history: Twitter Flight was released by Twitter in 2013 as a lightweight, component-based JavaScript framework. It was used internally at Twitter but never achieved broad adoption outside of Twitter's own apps. The project was effectively abandoned as Twitter moved to other frontend architectures.
Twitter Flight.summary_abuse: Twitter Flight is an abandoned JavaScript framework with no abuse signal.

Twitter typeahead.js.history: Twitter typeahead.js was released by Twitter in 2013 as a jQuery plugin for autocomplete/typeahead functionality. It was widely used for a time but the project was eventually abandoned as Twitter shifted away from jQuery-based tooling.
Twitter typeahead.js.summary_abuse: Twitter typeahead.js is an abandoned jQuery autocomplete library with no abuse signal.

Twsaa.history: Twsaa is a commerce platform with limited public history. No founding details, notable events, or acquisition history could be found.
Twsaa.summary_abuse: Twsaa is an obscure commerce platform with no notable abuse signal.

Twyne.history: Twyne is a lead management and optimization platform for lead distribution and monetization. It appears to serve performance marketing and affiliate networks. Limited public history is available.
Twyne.summary_abuse: Twyne is a lead generation and distribution platform. Lead gen platforms in performance marketing carry inherent risk of being used for fraudulent lead harvesting and spam, though Twyne is a minor player with no documented large-scale abuse.

Typecho.history: Typecho is a lightweight PHP blogging platform created by a Chinese developer around 2007. It is popular in China as a simple, self-hosted alternative to WordPress. It remains community-maintained with no commercial backing.
Typecho.summary_abuse: Typecho is a self-hosted PHP blogging platform with minimal abuse signal. Like any self-hosted CMS, installations can become compromised and used for malware hosting if not maintained, but the platform itself is not a primary fraud vector.

Typed.js.history: Typed.js is a JavaScript library for typewriter-style text animation effects, created by Matt Boldt and first released around 2014. It is MIT-licensed and widely used for animated text displays on landing pages.
Typed.js.summary_abuse: Typed.js is a typewriter animation library with no abuse signal. It is a UI effects library with no consumer-facing infrastructure.

TypeDoc.history: TypeDoc is an open-source API documentation generator for TypeScript, first released around 2015. It is community-maintained and widely used in the TypeScript ecosystem to generate HTML documentation from TypeScript source code and JSDoc comments.
TypeDoc.summary_abuse: TypeDoc is a TypeScript documentation generator with no abuse signal. It is a development toolchain utility with no consumer-facing surface.

Typeflo.history: Typeflo is a blogging platform that integrates with Webflow to enable blog publishing. It appears to be a small startup product launched around 2021.
Typeflo.summary_abuse: Typeflo is an obscure blogging platform with no notable abuse signal.

Typeform.history: Typeform was founded in 2012 in Barcelona, Spain. It grew to become a leading form and survey platform known for its conversational UI approach. Typeform raised significant venture capital from General Atlantic and others and remained independent. It reached unicorn valuation territory by the late 2010s.
Typeform.summary_abuse: Typeform is widely used for legitimate surveys, forms, and data collection. Its free tier and ease of embedding make it attractive for phishing credential-harvesting pages, and security researchers have documented Typeform forms being used in phishing campaigns to collect sensitive data. Typeform publishes an AUP and processes takedown reports, but the platform's flexibility creates ongoing abuse exposure.

Typekit.history: Typekit was founded in 2009 and acquired by Adobe in 2011. It became the web fonts component of Adobe's Creative Cloud platform. In 2018 Adobe rebranded Typekit as Adobe Fonts, making it available to all Creative Cloud subscribers.
Typekit.summary_abuse: Typekit (now Adobe Fonts) loads font files from Adobe's CDN and includes Adobe's tracking infrastructure. Font loading scripts can be used to track page visitors across domains. No significant fraud-specific abuse is documented for web font delivery, though the embedded scripts carry Adobe's analytics fingerprinting.

TypePad.history: TypePad was launched in October 2003 by Six Apart, built on the Movable Type blogging platform. Six Apart later merged with VideoEgg to form SAY Media, which sold TypePad to Endurance International Group. New signups were halted in 2020, and TypePad announced it would shut down service on September 30, 2025.
TypePad.summary_abuse: TypePad has no notable abuse signal beyond typical legacy blog platform spam. The service stopped accepting new signups in 2020 and shut down in September 2025, so its exposure is minimal and decreasing.

Typer.js.history: Typer.js is a small JavaScript library for typewriter text effects, created as an alternative to Typed.js. It is community-maintained with no commercial backing.
Typer.js.summary_abuse: Typer.js is a typewriter animation library with no abuse signal.

TypeScript.history: TypeScript was developed by Microsoft, with Anders Hejlsberg as lead architect, and first publicly released in October 2012. It is a superset of JavaScript that adds optional static typing and compiles to plain JavaScript. It became the de facto standard for large-scale JavaScript development and is used across Google, Microsoft, Airbnb, and most major software organizations.
TypeScript.summary_abuse: TypeScript is a programming language with no abuse signal. It is a development-time type checker that compiles to JavaScript and has no consumer-facing infrastructure.

Typesense.history: Typesense is an open-source search engine created by Jasonbosco and the Typesense team, first released in 2019 as a typo-tolerant, fast alternative to Elasticsearch and Algolia. It also offers a managed cloud service called Typesense Cloud.
Typesense.summary_abuse: Typesense is a backend search engine with no consumer-facing surface. It is not a target for phishing, spam, or fraud. No documented abuse incidents are associated with Typesense.

Typo 00.history: Typo00 is a digital ordering system for restaurants, likely based in Germany. Limited public history is available.
Typo 00.summary_abuse: Typo00 is a restaurant digital ordering system with no notable abuse signal.

TYPO3 CMS.history: TYPO3 was created by Kasper Skarhoj in Denmark in 1997-1998 and became one of the dominant PHP CMSes in the European enterprise market, particularly in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland. The TYPO3 Association (a nonprofit foundation) was formed to steward the project. It remains active and widely used in DACH enterprise environments.
TYPO3 CMS.summary_abuse: TYPO3 has minimal abuse signal. It is a self-hosted enterprise CMS with historical CVEs like XSS and SQL injection in older versions, but the security team maintains an active advisory program. It has no consumer-facing hosted surface that scammers exploit directly.

Typof.history: Typof is an Indian e-commerce platform for artisans and small sellers to create online stores, founded around 2016. It serves the Indian market with premium website and selling features for craftspeople.
Typof.summary_abuse: Typof is a small Indian e-commerce platform with no notable abuse signal beyond the general risk of any e-commerce platform hosting potentially fraudulent storefronts.

Tyslo EasySell.history: Tyslo EasySell is a Shopify app that replaces the default checkout with an embedded or popup order form, designed to increase conversion rates. Limited public history is available.
